title: Contact sport

infobox.value: is any [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") where physical contact between competitors, or their environment, is an integral part of the game. Examples include [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"), [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), and [acrobatic](/wiki/Acrobatic "Acrobatic") sports. Contact may come about as the result of intentional or incidental actions by the players in the course of play. This is in contrast to non-contact sports where players often have no opportunity to make contact with each other and the laws of the game may expressly forbid contact. In contact sports some forms of contact are encouraged as a critical aspect of the game such as [tackling](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)"), while others are incidental such as when shielding the ball or contesting an aerial challenge. As the types of contact between players is not equal between all sports they define the types of contact that is deemed acceptable and fall within the laws of the game, while outlawing other types of physical contact that might be considered expressly dangerous or risky such as a [high tackle](/wiki/High_tackle "High tackle") or [spear tackle](/wiki/Spear_tackle "Spear tackle"), or against the spirit of the game such as striking [below the belt](/wiki/Below_the_belt "Below the belt") or other [unsportsmanlike conduct](/wiki/Unsportsmanlike_conduct "Unsportsmanlike conduct"). Where there is a limit as to how much contact is acceptable most sports have a mechanism to call a [foul](/wiki/Foul_(sports) "Foul (sports)") by the referee, umpire or similar official when an offence is deemed to have occurred.

is primarily any combat sport that allows the competitor to attempt to knock-out or otherwise defeat their opponent by physically incapacitating them. Examples of this would include most professional martial arts such as [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and some forms of karate and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"). Defeating an opponent generally takes place using in isolation or combination actions such as striking and grappling depending on the rules of the sport.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

is typically a [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") involving [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") and containing physical contact between the combatants simulating full-power techniques. The techniques are restricted to limited power and rendering the opponent [unconscious](/wiki/Unconsciousness "Unconsciousness") is forbidden. Some semi-contact sports use a point system to determine the winner and use extensive protective gear to protect the athletes from injury. Examples of semi-contact sports include [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu"), [Kenpo](/wiki/American_Kenpo "American Kenpo"), various [Korean martial arts](/wiki/Korean_martial_arts "Korean martial arts") that incorporate contact rules sparring, [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo"), some types of [historical European martial arts](/wiki/Historical_European_martial_arts "Historical European martial arts"), [fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing") and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sport involving physical contact between players or their environment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/College_football_TT_USNA.jpg/250px-College_football_TT_USNA.jpg)](/wiki/File:College_football_TT_USNA.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Holender_and_Yanda%C5%9F_in_the_international_match_%28September_2020%29.jpg/250px-Holender_and_Yanda%C5%9F_in_the_international_match_%28September_2020%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holender_and_Yanda%C5%9F_in_the_international_match_(September_2020).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg/250px-Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_vs_Belgium_rugby_match.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Caddy_tackling_Pendlebury_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Caddy_tackling_Pendlebury_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caddy_tackling_Pendlebury_(cropped).jpg)

Various major contact sports.  
Clockwise:  
[Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union")  
[American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")  
[Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (soccer)  
[Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football")

A **contact sport** is any [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") where physical contact between competitors, or their environment, is an integral part of the game. Examples include [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"), [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), and [acrobatic](/wiki/Acrobatic "Acrobatic") sports. Contact may come about as the result of intentional or incidental actions by the players in the course of play. This is in contrast to non-contact sports where players often have no opportunity to make contact with each other and the laws of the game may expressly forbid contact. In contact sports some forms of contact are encouraged as a critical aspect of the game such as [tackling](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)"), while others are incidental such as when shielding the ball or contesting an aerial challenge. As the types of contact between players is not equal between all sports they define the types of contact that is deemed acceptable and fall within the laws of the game, while outlawing other types of physical contact that might be considered expressly dangerous or risky such as a [high tackle](/wiki/High_tackle "High tackle") or [spear tackle](/wiki/Spear_tackle "Spear tackle"), or against the spirit of the game such as striking [below the belt](/wiki/Below_the_belt "Below the belt") or other [unsportsmanlike conduct](/wiki/Unsportsmanlike_conduct "Unsportsmanlike conduct"). Where there is a limit as to how much contact is acceptable most sports have a mechanism to call a [foul](/wiki/Foul_(sports) "Foul (sports)") by the referee, umpire or similar official when an offence is deemed to have occurred.

Contact sports are categorised by the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) into three main categories: contact, limited-contact and noncontact. In attempting to define relative risk for competitors in sports the AAP have further defined contact sports as containing some element of intentional collision between players.[[1]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-27403556-1)[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2) They define such collision sports as being where: "athletes purposely hit or collide with each other or with inanimate objects (including the ground) with great force", while in limited-contact sports such impacts are often "infrequent and inadvertent".[[1]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-27403556-1) While contact sports are considered the most high risk for injury, in some sports being a major feature (such as boxing or other martial arts), limited-contact and non-contact sports are not without risk as injury or contact may come about as a result of a fall or collision with the playing area, or a piece of [sporting equipment](/wiki/Sporting_equipment "Sporting equipment"), such as being struck by a [hockey stick](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick") or [football](/wiki/Football_(ball) "Football (ball)") or even a piece of protective wear worn by a teammate or opposition player.

Some definitions of contact sports, particularly martial arts, have the concept of full contact, semi-contact and non-contact (or other definitions) for both training and competitive sparring. Some categories of contact may or may not be combined with other methods of scoring, but full contact is generally considered to include the potential for victory by knockout or submission depending on the form of combat.[[3]](#cite_note-WFMAF-3)[[4]](#cite_note-WMAC_Sparring-4)[[5]](#cite_note-WMAC_Contact_Rules-5) An example of this is [full contact karate](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate") and taekwondo where competitors are allowed to attempt to knock out an opponent through strikes to the head or otherwise bringing the opponent to the floor.[[6]](#cite_note-BJSMTaekwondo.-6)[[7]](#cite_note-UdoMoenigOrigins-7) Full contact rules differentiate from other forms of the same sports that may otherwise restrict blows to the head and the use of elbows or knees.[[5]](#cite_note-WMAC_Contact_Rules-5) Such full contact sports may be defined as [combat sports](/wiki/Combat_sports "Combat sports") and require a of change equipment, alter or omit rules, and are generally differentiated from contact sports by their explicit intent of defeating an opponent in physical combat.[[8]](#cite_note-UdoMoenigSparring-8)[[3]](#cite_note-WFMAF-3)[[5]](#cite_note-WMAC_Contact_Rules-5)

Some contact sports have limited-contact or non-contact variations (such as [flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football") for [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")) which attempt to replace tackling and other forms of contact with alternative methods of interacting with an opponent, such as removing a flag from a belt worn by the opponent or outlawing specific actions entirely such as in [walking football](/wiki/Walking_football "Walking football").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

Current medical terminology in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") uses the term contact sport and collision sport to refer to sports like [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse") and [roller derby](/wiki/Roller_derby "Roller derby"). The term limited-contact sport is used to refer to sports such as [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") and [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), and the term noncontact sport to sports like [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [running](/wiki/Running "Running") and [swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming").[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2)

The [American Academy of Pediatrics](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Pediatrics "American Academy of Pediatrics") policy statement was revised in 2008 to include the following definition:

> In collision sports (e.g. [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), ice hockey, American football, lacrosse, and [rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo "Rodeo")), athletes purposely hit or collide with each other or with inanimate objects (including the ground) with great force. In contact sports (e.g. basketball), athletes routinely make contact with each other or with inanimate objects but usually with less force than in collision sports. In limited-contact sports (e.g. [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") and squash), contact with other athletes or with inanimate objects is infrequent or inadvertent.[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2)

## Grades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Grades")]

### Full-contact martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Full-contact martial arts")]

Main article: [Combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport")

A **full contact sport** is primarily any combat sport that allows the competitor to attempt to knock-out or otherwise defeat their opponent by physically incapacitating them. Examples of this would include most professional martial arts such as [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and some forms of karate and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"). Defeating an opponent generally takes place using in isolation or combination actions such as striking and grappling depending on the rules of the sport.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Full-contact [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") include:

* [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing")
* [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu")
* [Judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo")
* Various forms of [Full contact karate](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate")
* [Kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing")
* [Lethwei](/wiki/Lethwei "Lethwei")
* [Mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts")
* [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai")
* [Pradal serey](/wiki/Pradal_serey "Pradal serey")
* [Sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)")
* [Sanda](/wiki/Sanda_(sport) "Sanda (sport)")
* [Savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate")
* [Sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo")
* [Taekkyon](/wiki/Taekkyon "Taekkyon")
* Some forms of [Taekwondo](/wiki/World_Taekwondo#Sparring "World Taekwondo")
* [Vovinam](/wiki/Vovinam "Vovinam")
* [Wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling")

### Semi-contact martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Semi-contact martial arts")]

A **semi-contact sport** is typically a [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") involving [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") and containing physical contact between the combatants simulating full-power techniques. The techniques are restricted to limited power and rendering the opponent [unconscious](/wiki/Unconsciousness "Unconsciousness") is forbidden. Some semi-contact sports use a point system to determine the winner and use extensive protective gear to protect the athletes from injury. Examples of semi-contact sports include [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu"), [Kenpo](/wiki/American_Kenpo "American Kenpo"), various [Korean martial arts](/wiki/Korean_martial_arts "Korean martial arts") that incorporate contact rules sparring, [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo"), some types of [historical European martial arts](/wiki/Historical_European_martial_arts "Historical European martial arts"), [fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing") and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Contact sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Contact sports")]

As defined by the AAP and also considered collision sports.[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2) The AAP notes Martial arts can be subclassified as judo, jujitsu, karate, kung fu, and tae kwon do; some forms are contact sports and others are limited-contact sports.

* [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")
* [Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")
* [Australian football](/wiki/Australian_football "Australian football")
* [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing")

### Limited-contact sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Limited-contact sports")]

As defined by the AAP.[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2) The AAP notes Martial arts can be subclassified as judo, jujitsu, karate, kung fu, and tae kwon do; some forms are contact sports and others are limited-contact sports.

* [Adventure racing](/wiki/Adventure_racing "Adventure racing")
* [Baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball")
* [Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball")
* [Bicycling](/wiki/Bicycling "Bicycling")
* [Cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)")
* [Fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing")
* [Field events](/wiki/Throwing_sports "Throwing sports")
* [Floor hockey](/wiki/Floor_hockey "Floor hockey")
* [Football, flag or touch](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football")
* [Futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal")
* [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football")
* [Gaelic handball](/wiki/Gaelic_handball "Gaelic handball")
* [Handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball")
* [High jump](/wiki/High_jump "High jump")
* [Horseback riding](/wiki/Horseback_riding "Horseback riding")
* [Ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating")
* [Inline skating](/wiki/Inline_skating "Inline skating")
* [Martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts")
* [Pole vault](/wiki/Pole_vault "Pole vault")
* [Racquetball](/wiki/Racquetball "Racquetball")
* [Roller skating](/wiki/Roller_skating "Roller skating")

* [Surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") or [windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing")
* [Volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball")
* [Water skiing](/wiki/Water_skiing "Water skiing")
* [Weightlifting](/wiki/Weightlifting "Weightlifting")
* [Whitewater canoeing](/wiki/Whitewater_canoeing "Whitewater canoeing") or [whitewater kayaking](/wiki/Whitewater_kayaking "Whitewater kayaking")

[Tag](/wiki/Tag_(game)#Tag_competitions "Tag (game)"), while rarely an organized sport, could also be considered a semi-contact sport.

### Non-contact sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non-contact sports")]

As defined by the AAP.[[2]](#cite_note-ncbi-pubmed-18381550-2)

* [Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton")
* [Bodybuilding](/wiki/Bodybuilding "Bodybuilding")
* [Bowling](/wiki/Bowling "Bowling")
* [Canoeing](/wiki/Canoeing "Canoeing") or [kayaking](/wiki/Kayaking "Kayaking") (flatwater)
* [Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket")
* [Curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling")
* [Dance](/wiki/Dance "Dance")
* [Discus](/wiki/Discus "Discus")
* [Field events](/wiki/Field_events "Field events")
* [Golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf")
* [Javelin](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw")
* [Netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball")

## Sports injury and prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sports injury and prevention")]

Main article: [Sports injury](/wiki/Sports_injury "Sports injury")

### Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Equipment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/David_Backes.JPG/250px-David_Backes.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Backes.JPG)

[St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues") player [David Backes](/wiki/David_Backes "David Backes") with [ice hockey helmet](/wiki/Ice_hockey_helmet "Ice hockey helmet").

As a result of the risk of injury, some sports require the use of [protective equipment](/wiki/Protective_gear_in_sports "Protective gear in sports"), for example [American football protective equipment](/wiki/Protective_equipment_in_gridiron_football "Protective equipment in gridiron football") or the gloves and helmets needed for [underwater hockey](/wiki/Underwater_hockey "Underwater hockey"). Some sports are also played on soft ground and have padding on physical obstacles, such as [goal posts](/wiki/Goal_(sports) "Goal (sports)").

Most contact sports require any male players to wear a [protective cup](/wiki/Jockstrap#Protective_cup "Jockstrap") to protect their genitalia.

There has been an increasing medical, academic, and media focus on sports involving rapid contact in the late 20th to early 21st century and their relationship with sports injuries. Several sports' governing bodies began changing their rules in order to decrease the incidence of serious injuries and avoid lawsuits. In some countries, new laws have been passed, particularly in regard to concussions.

To reduce concussions in American football, the National Football League implemented the use of [Guardian Caps](/wiki/Guardian_Cap "Guardian Cap").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Guardian Caps are soft-shelled helmets that have a soft material covering the outside of the helmet that absorb the initial intensity of the impact. These caps are worn in practice and in games by different football levels, ranging from youth athletes to those in the National Football League. According to the NFL, because of the Guardian Cap NXT, concussions have decreased by 52%.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Concussion protocols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Concussion protocols")]

At the professional level, America's professional football league, the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), implemented The [Concussion](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion") Protocol in 2011, banning concussed players from re-entering the same game in which they were injured in order to reduce the risk of further injury and damage.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In Canada in 2018, [Rowan's Law](/w/index.php?title=Rowan%27s_Law&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rowan's Law (page does not exist)") was passed after the death of a young Canadian female athlete. [Rowan Stringer](/w/index.php?title=Rowan_Stringer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rowan Stringer (page does not exist)") died in 2013 of [second-impact syndrome](/wiki/Second-impact_syndrome "Second-impact syndrome"), "...the result of suffering multiple concussions playing rugby three times in six days."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Doctors and sports physical therapists can use ImPACT tests[[13]](#cite_note-13) to test athletes post-concussion, and help them with a speedy recovery. This is conducted through cognitive tests, verbal and visual memory activities, and reaction time. These tests are done to make sure that the athletes are cleared to play, and won't be at risk for another concussion.

### Long-term Effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contact_sport&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Long-term Effects")]

There are two main long term effects caused by ongoing concussions in contact sports, CTE ([Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy](/wiki/Chronic_traumatic_encephalopathy "Chronic traumatic encephalopathy")) and PCS ([Post-Concussion Syndrome](/wiki/Post-concussion_syndrome "Post-concussion syndrome"))[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14). Studies on retired and deceased former players have found signs of increased rates of CTE, [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), and other, often incurable, neurological diseases.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Symptoms of long term effects include increased irritability, anxiety, processing speed, and difficulties with memory. [[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)
